devanagari: पूतनावधः । श्रीशुक उवाच । नन्दः पथि वचः शौरेः न मृषेति विचिन्तयन् । हरिं जगाम शरणं उत्पातागमशङ्‍कितः ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: pūtanāvadhaḥ śrīśuka uvāca nandaḥ pathi vacaḥ śaureḥ na mṛṣeti vicintayan hariṃ jagāma śaraṇaṃ utpātāgamaśaṅ‍kitaḥ

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
On his way (back to Gokula), Nanda pondered that the words of Vasudeva might not be untrue. Being apprehensive of impending calamities, (mentally) he sought refuge in Lord Hari.

devanagari: कंसेन प्रहिता घोरा पूतना बालघातिनी । शिशूंश्चचार निघ्नन्ती पुरग्रामव्रजादिषु ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: kaṃsena prahitā ghorā pūtanā bālaghātinī śiśūṃścacāra nighnantī puragrāmavrajādiṣu

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: Deputed by Kaṃsa, the terrible, infant-killer Pūtan moved about, killing babes and infants in cities, towns, cowherd-settlements (on her way).

devanagari: न यत्र श्रवणादीनि रक्षोघ्नानि स्वकर्मसु । कुर्वन्ति सात्वतां भर्तुः यातुधान्यश्च तत्र हि ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: na yatra śravaṇādīni rakṣoghnāni svakarmasu kurvanti sātvatāṃ bhartuḥ yātudhānyaśca tatra hi

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

translation: Wherever, people, while attending to their duties, do not listen to (recite etc.) the names and deeds of the Lord of Sātvatas (Kṛṣṇa) which are capable of annihilating demons, there and there only, ogresses (and other evil spirits) prevail.

devanagari: सा खेचर्येकदोत्पत्य पूतना नन्दगोकुलम् । योषित्वा माययाऽत्मानं प्राविशत् कामचारिणी ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: sā khecaryekadotpatya pūtanā nandagokulam yoṣitvā māyayā
translation: One day, that Pūtanā who was capable of coursing through the sky, flew (through the sky) to the Gokula ruled by Nanda. Assuming the form of a young beautiful woman through her witchcraft, she entered the Gokula as the wantonness was capable of assuming any guise, and moving into any house she pleased.

devanagari: तां केशबन्ध व्यतिषक्तमल्लिकां । बृहन्नितंब स्तनकृच्छ्रमध्यमाम् । सुवाससं कल्पितकर्णभूषण । त्विषोल्लसत् कुन्तलमण्डिताननाम् ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: tāṃ keśabandha vyatiṣaktamallikāṃ bṛhannitaṃba stanakṛcchramadhyamām suvāsasaṃ kalpitakarṇabhūṣaṇa tviṣollasat kuntalamaṇḍitānanām

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative plural from √ maṇḍ class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ maṇḍ ]

translation: On seeing that beautiful damsel the braids of whose hair were adorned with jasmine flowers (interwoven therein), whose slender waist was (as if) crushed between her big buttocks and heavy breasts, who was clad in rich garments, whose face was beautified with the locks of hair lustrous with the radiance of the brilliant swaying ear-rings, who with her charming smiles and sidelong glances captivated the hearts of the inhabitants of Vraja (who hence failed to extern her), and whom, due to her wearing a blooming lotus in hand, the Gopa women thought to be goddess Lakṣmī incarnate, who had come to see her Lord (and they kept silent in awe and respect, instead of barring her entrance).

devanagari: वल्गुस्मितापाङ्‍ग विसर्गवीक्षितैः । मनो हरन्तीं वनितां व्रजौकसाम् । अमंसताम्भोजकरेण रूपिणीं । गोप्यः श्रियं द्रष्टुमिवागतां पतिम् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: valgusmitāpāṅ‍ga visargavīkṣitaiḥ mano harantīṃ vanitāṃ vrajaukasām amaṃsatāmbhojakareṇa rūpiṇīṃ gopyaḥ śriyaṃ draṣṭumivāgatāṃ patim

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active third dual], [imperative middle third single]

translation: On seeing that beautiful damsel the braids of whose hair were adorned with jasmine flowers (interwoven therein), whose slender waist was (as if) crushed between her big buttocks and heavy breasts, who was clad in rich garments, whose face was beautified with the locks of hair lustrous with the radiance of the brilliant swaying ear-rings, who with her charming smiles and sidelong glances captivated the hearts of the inhabitants of Vraja (who hence failed to extern her), and whom, due to her wearing a blooming lotus in hand, the Gopa women thought to be goddess Lakṣmī incarnate, who had come to see her Lord (and they kept silent in awe and respect, instead of barring her entrance).

devanagari: बालग्रहस्तत्र विचिन्वती शिशून् । यदृच्छया नन्दगृहेऽसदन्तकम् । बालं प्रतिच्छन्ननिजोरुतेजसं । ददर्श तल्पेऽग्निमिवाहितं भसि ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: bālagrahastatra vicinvatī śiśūn yadṛcchayā nandagṛhe bālaṃ praticchannanijorutejasaṃ dadarśa talpe

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: Pūtanā, that infant-killer evil spirit, roving in search for babies, came by chance to the house of Nanda, and saw the child Kṛṣṇa who was the exterminator of the wicked, but was lying in bed with his immeasurable innate glory veiled, like fire covered under ashes.

devanagari: विबुध्य तां बालक मारिकाग्रहं । चराचरात्मा स निमीलितेक्षणः । अनन्तमारोपयदङ्कमन्तकं । यथोरगं सुप्तमबुद्धिरज्जुधीः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: vibudhya tāṃ bālaka mārikāgrahaṃ carācarātmā sa nimīlitekṣaṇaḥ anantamāropayadaṅkamantakaṃ yathoragaṃ suptamabuddhirajjudhīḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]
[nominative single from √ svap class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ svap class 2 verb]

translation:
Recognizing her to be the evil spirit which kills infants, the Lord of the mobile and immobile creation lay quiet with his eyes closed[1]. And she placed the Infinite Lord, her very death, on her lap, as a stupid person would do to a sleeping serpent, mistaking it for a rope.
8(A). Even though he was the Supreme Ruler of the universe the Lord, playing the part of a child, spoke nothing. But the fiendess was unaware that he was the lord Hari who by his very nature knows directly all the activities of the world.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Padaratnāvalī’s text intersperses one verse here.

devanagari: तां तीक्ष्णचित्तामतिवामचेष्टितां । वीक्ष्यान्तरा कोषपरिच्छदासिवत् । वरस्त्रियं तत्प्रभया च धर्षिते । निरीक्ष्यमाणे जननी ह्यतिष्ठताम् ॥ ९...

translation: Finding suddenly within their house that superb accomplished lady behaving (externally) most affectionately and in a charming way, even though her heart was sharp (cruel like sword encased in a charming scabbard, both the mothers (Yaśodā and Rohiṇī) were stupefied with her effulgence and stood agazing (instead of preventing her).

devanagari: तस्मिन् स्तनं दुर्जरवीर्यमुल्बणं । घोराङ्कमादाय शिशोर्ददावथ । गाढं कराभ्यां भगवान् प्रपीड्य तत् । प्राणैः समं रोषसमन्वितोऽपिबत् ॥ १० ॥

translation: The terrible demoness placed the child Kṛṣṇa on her lap and suckled him at her breast full of indigestible virulent poison. Thereupon, with indignation, the Lord squeezed hard her breast with his hands and sucked it along with her life.

devanagari: सा मुञ्च मुञ्चालमिति प्रभाषिणी । निष्पीड्य मानाखिलजीवमर्मणि । विवृत्य नेत्रे चरणौ भुजौ मुहुः । प्रस्विन्नगात्रा क्षिपती रुरोद ह ॥ ११ ॥

translation: Being crushed in all her vital parts, she cried out “Let me go, enough of it, leave me”. With her body drenched in perspiration and with her hands and feet thrown in convulsions and with distended eyes, she wailed loudly.

devanagari: तस्याः स्वनेनातिगभीररंहसा । साद्रिर्मही द्यौश्च चचाल सग्रहा । रसा दिशश्च प्रतिनेदिरे जनाः । पेतुः क्षितौ वज्रनिपात शङ्कया ॥ १२ ॥

translation: By her extremely loud and deep cry, the earth along with the mountains, and the heavens along with the planets, were shaken. The netherworld and all the cardinal points were reverberated with the Sound. People being afraid of the discharge of Vajra, fell down on the ground.

devanagari: निशाचरीत्थं व्यथितस्तना व्यसुः । व्यादाय केशांश्चरणौ भुजावपि । प्रसार्य गोष्ठे निजरूपमास्थिता । वज्राहतो वृत्र इवापतन्नृप ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: niśācarītthaṃ vyathitastanā vyasuḥ vyādāya keśāṃścaraṇau bhujāvapi prasārya goṣṭhe nijarūpamāsthitā vajrāhato vṛtra ivāpatannṛpa

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ vyath class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ vyath ]

translation: Oh King, the demoness who, being excruciatingly afflicted at the breast, thus fell dead, reverting to her original form with her mouth agape and her hair, hands and feet stretched near the cowpen even as Vṛtra fell when struck down with Vajra (thunderbolt).

devanagari: पतमानोऽपि तद्देह त्रिगव्यूत्यन्तरद्रुमान् । चूर्णयामास राजेन्द्र महदासीत् तदद्‍भुतम् ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: patamāno cūrṇayāmāsa rājendra mahadāsīt tadad‍bhutam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [aorist active third single], [injunctive active third single]

translation: Even while it fell, her (giant) body crushed to powder trees within an area of twelve miles. It was the most astonishing sight, Oh king of kings.

devanagari: ईषामात्रोग्रदंष्ट्रास्यं गिरिकन्दर नासिकम् । गण्डशैलस्तनं रौद्रं प्रकीर्णारुणमूर्धजम् ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: īṣāmātrogradaṃṣṭrāsyaṃ girikandara nāsikam gaṇḍaśailastanaṃ raudraṃ prakīrṇāruṇamūrdhajam
translation: The cowherds—men and women—whose hearts, ears and heads were already rent asunder with the terrible roars and yells of the demoness, were struck with terror to see her fearful body with a mouth raged with a terrible set of tusks long like the poles of ploughs, with nostrils resembling mountain caverns; breasts huge like (a pair of hillocks, her reddish hair terribly dishevelled, her eyes deep like dark wells, her buttock terrifically big like sand dunes, her arms, legs and thighs resembling dams (around) her belly looking like a dried up, waterless pool.

devanagari: अन्धकूपगभीराक्षं पुलिनारोह भीषणम् । बद्धसेतुभुजोर्वङ्घ्रि शून्यतोय ह्रदोदरम् ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: andhakūpagabhīrākṣaṃ pulināroha bhīṣaṇam baddhasetubhujorvaṅghri śūnyatoya hradodaram
translation: The cowherds—men and women—whose hearts, ears and heads were already rent asunder with the terrible roars and yells of the demoness, were struck with terror to see her fearful body with a mouth raged with a terrible set of tusks long like the poles of ploughs, with nostrils resembling mountain caverns; breasts huge like (a pair of hillocks, her reddish hair terribly dishevelled, her eyes deep like dark wells, her buttock terrifically big like sand dunes, her arms, legs and thighs resembling dams (around) her belly looking like a dried up, waterless pool.

devanagari: सन्तत्रसुः स्म तद्वीक्ष्य गोपा गोप्यः कलेवरम् । पूर्वं तु तन्निःस्वनित भिन्नहृत्कर्ण मस्तकाः ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: santatrasuḥ sma tadvīkṣya gopā gopyaḥ kalevaram pūrvaṃ tu tanniḥsvanita bhinnahṛtkarṇa mastakāḥ

translation: The cowherds—men and women—whose hearts, ears and heads were already rent asunder with the terrible roars and yells of the demoness, were struck with terror to see her fearful body with a mouth raged with a terrible set of tusks long like the poles of ploughs, with nostrils resembling mountain caverns; breasts huge like (a pair of hillocks, her reddish hair terribly dishevelled, her eyes deep like dark wells, her buttock terrifically big like sand dunes, her arms, legs and thighs resembling dams (around) her belly looking like a dried up, waterless pool.

devanagari: बालं च तस्या उरसि क्रीडन्तं अकुतोभयम् । गोप्यस्तूर्णं समभ्येत्य जगृहुर्जातसंभ्रमाः ॥ १८ ॥

translation: Seeing the child playing fearlessly on her bosom, the cowherd-women, in their bewilderment, quickly came to him and picked him up.

devanagari: यशोदा रोहिणीभ्यां ताः समं बालस्य सर्वतः । रक्षां विदधिरे सम्यक् गोपुच्छभ्रमणादिभिः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: yaśodā rohiṇībhyāṃ tāḥ samaṃ bālasya sarvataḥ rakṣāṃ vidadhire samyak gopucchabhramaṇādibhiḥ
translation: They, also with Yaśodā and Rohiṇī ensured the (future) safety of the child on all sides, against evil spirits (and such other mishaps) by performing the ceremony of waving of the cow’s tail round the child and other rites.

devanagari: गोमूत्रेण स्नापयित्वा पुनर्गोरजसार्भकम् । रक्षां चक्रुश्च शकृता द्वादशाङ्गेषु नामभिः ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: gomūtreṇa snāpayitvā punargorajasārbhakam rakṣāṃ cakruśca śakṛtā dvādaśāṅgeṣu nāmabhiḥ
translation: They bathed the child with the urine of a cow and besmeared it with dust raised by her hoofs. And by applying cowdungs to twelve (different) parts of his body[2] pronouncing simultaneously twelve (different) names of the Lord, they ensured his safety (by this spiritual talisman).
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Subodhinī enumerates the parts of the body to be so daubed with cow-dung, as follows:
lalāṭam bāhumūle ca hṛdayam nābhi-pārśvakam /
kaṇṭhaḥ skandhau kaṭir mūrdhā stanau ceti vidur budhāḥ //
Forehead, arms, cheat, navel, throat, shoulders, waist, head and the nipples on the chest. And names of god Viṣṇu to be uttered are the first twelve names out of the twenty-four names uttered at the beginning of Sandhyā vandana.
VT. quotes from the Padma Purāṇa the procedure which is as follows:—
“The particular aspect of the Lord is visualized as consecrating and abiding in that particular part of the body.”

devanagari: गोप्यः संस्पृष्टसलिला अङ्गेषु करयोः पृथक् । न्यस्यात्मन्यथ बालस्य बीजन्यासमकुर्वत ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: gopyaḥ saṃspṛṣṭasalilā aṅgeṣu karayoḥ pṛthak nyasyātmanyatha bālasya bījanyāsamakurvata
translation:
(The Gopa women, being bewildered, forgot that they have to get themselves so consecrated before applying the talisman to the child. When they recovered....)
They performed the ācamana (sipping of water uttering the names of God for self-purification), made the bīja-nyāsa (assignment of the various parts of the body to deities accompanied with the uttering of some ‘seed-letters’ and corresponding gesticulations) first on both of their hands and parts of their body, and then followed the same procedure of the ‘placement of seed-letters’ on the body of the child (as follows:).
(The Gopa women said)

devanagari: अव्यादजोऽङ्घ्रि मणिमांस्तव जान्वथोरू । यज्ञोऽच्युतः कटितटं जठरं हयास्यः । हृत्केशवस्त्वदुर ईश इनस्तु कण्ठं । विष्णुर्भुजं मुखमुरुक्रम ई...
english_devnagari: avyādajo yajño hṛtkeśavastvadura īśa inastu kaṇṭhaṃ viṣṇurbhujaṃ mukhamurukrama īśvaraḥ kam

[ablative single from √ u class 1 verb], [ablative single from √ u class 2 verb], [ablative single from √ u class 5 verb]

translation: “May Aja (the birthless Lord) protect your feet! May Maṇiman[3] (the Indweller of the jīva.) guard your knees; Yajña, your thighs; Acyuta, your loins; Hayagrīva (horsenecked Lord), your abdomen; Keśava, your heart; Iśa [Īśa?] (the Supreme Ruler), your bosom; Ina (The Lord), your throat; Viṣṇu, your arms; Urukrama, your mouth; and Īśvara (the Supreme Lord), your head.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
v.l. maṇiman—the wearer of the Kaustubha gem—KD.

devanagari: चक्र्यग्रतः सहगदो हरिरस्तु पश्चात् । त्वत्पार्श्वयोर्धनुरसी मधुहाजनश्च । कोणेषु शङ्ख उरुगाय उपर्युपेन्द्रः । तार्क्ष्यः क्षितौ हलधरः पु...
english_devnagari: cakryagrataḥ sahagado harirastu paścāt tvatpārśvayordhanurasī madhuhājanaśca koṇeṣu śaṅkha urugāya uparyupendraḥ tārkṣyaḥ kṣitau haladharaḥ puruṣaḥ samantāt

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ kṣī class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ kṣī class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ kṣī class 1 verb], [nominative dual from √ kṣī class 5 verb], [vocative dual from √ kṣī class 5 verb], [accusative dual from √ kṣī class 5 verb], [nominative dual from √ kṣī class 9 verb], [vocative dual from √ kṣī class 9 verb], [accusative dual from √ kṣī class 9 verb]

translation: May the wielder of (Sudarśana) discus protect you at the front, and the Bearer of the (Kaumodakī) mace, at the back; May the slayer of Madhu and Ajana (the birthless Lord) each bearing a bow and a sword, protect you on both the sides; May Urugāya (the Lord sung by many) bearing the conch (Pāñcajanya), at the four corners (points intervening between quarters); Upendra (Vāmana), overhead; Tārkṣya, on the ground and the Supreme Man wielding a plough, on all sides.

devanagari: इन्द्रियाणि हृषीकेशः प्राणान्नारायणोऽवतु । श्वेतद्वीपपतिश्चित्तं मनो योगेश्वरोऽवतु ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: indriyāṇi hṛṣīkeśaḥ prāṇānnārāyaṇo śvetadvīpapatiścittaṃ mano yogeśvaro
translation: May Hṛṣīkeśa protect your senses; Nārāyaṇa, your vital breaths; the Lord of Śvetadvīpa, your heart (the seat of intellect); the Lord of Yoga, your mind.

devanagari: पृश्निगर्भस्तु ते बुद्धिं आत्मानं भगवान्परः । क्रीडन्तं पातु गोविन्दः शयानं पातु माधवः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: pṛśnigarbhastu te buddhiṃ ātmānaṃ bhagavānparaḥ krīḍantaṃ pātu govindaḥ śayānaṃ pātu mādhavaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ śī class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ śī class 2 verb]

translation: May the son of Pṛśni protect Your understanding; the Supreme Lord, Your ahaṃkāra (ego); may Govinda protect You while You are playing; Mādhava, while sleeping.

devanagari: व्रजन्तमव्याद् वैकुण्ठ आसीनं त्वां श्रियः पतिः । भुञ्जानं यज्ञभुक् पातु सर्वग्रहभयङ्करः ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: vrajantamavyād vaikuṇṭha āsīnaṃ tvāṃ śriyaḥ patiḥ bhuñjānaṃ yajñabhuk pātu sarvagrahabhayaṅkaraḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [ablative single from √ u class 1 verb], [ablative single from √ u class 2 verb], [ablative single from √ u class 5 verb]
[ablative single from √ u class 1 verb], [ablative single from √ u class 2 verb], [ablative single from √ u class 5 verb]

translation: May Vaikuṇṭha guard You while walking; the Lord of Śrī, while sitting. The enjoyer of sacrifices, the terror to all evil spirits, while (You are) eating.

devanagari: डाकिन्यो यातुधान्यश्च कुष्माण्डा येऽर्भकग्रहाः । भूतप्रेत पिशाचाश्च यक्षरक्षो विनायकाः ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: ḍākinyo yātudhānyaśca kuṣmāṇḍā ye bhūtapreta piśācāśca yakṣarakṣo vināyakāḥ
translation: (All evil spirits like) Dākinī, Yātudhāni, Kūṣmāṇḍas, the wicked spirits troubling young infants, Bhūtas, Pretas, Piśācas, Yakṣas, Rākṣasas and Vināyakas, Koṭarā, Revatī, Jyeṣṭhā, Pūtanā, Mātṛkās and others; Unmādas (spirits causing insanity,) and Apasmāras which trouble the body, senses and vital breaths; the evil spirits that frighten in dreams, the great calamitous portents, and those wicked spirits that seize the old and the young all these which are terrified at the mention of Viṣṇu’s name—may all these wicked spirits be destroyed.”

devanagari: कोटरा रेवती ज्येष्ठा पूतना मातृकादयः । उन्मादा ये ह्यपस्मारा देह प्राणेन्द्रियद्रुहः ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: koṭarā revatī jyeṣṭhā pūtanā mātṛkādayaḥ unmādā ye hyapasmārā deha prāṇendriyadruhaḥ
translation: (All evil spirits like) Dākinī, Yātudhāni, Kūṣmāṇḍas, the wicked spirits troubling young infants, Bhūtas, Pretas, Piśācas, Yakṣas, Rākṣasas and Vināyakas, Koṭarā, Revatī, Jyeṣṭhā, Pūtanā, Mātṛkās and others; Unmādas (spirits causing insanity,) and Apasmāras which trouble the body, senses and vital breaths; the evil spirits that frighten in dreams, the great calamitous portents, and those wicked spirits that seize the old and the young all these which are terrified at the mention of Viṣṇu’s name—may all these wicked spirits be destroyed.”

devanagari: स्वप्नदृष्टा महोत्पाता वृद्धा बालग्रहाश्च ये । सर्वे नश्यन्तु ते विष्णोः नामग्रहणभीरवः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: svapnadṛṣṭā mahotpātā vṛddhā bālagrahāśca ye sarve naśyantu te viṣṇoḥ nāmagrahaṇabhīravaḥ

translation: (All evil spirits like) Dākinī, Yātudhāni, Kūṣmāṇḍas, the wicked spirits troubling young infants, Bhūtas, Pretas, Piśācas, Yakṣas, Rākṣasas and Vināyakas, Koṭarā, Revatī, Jyeṣṭhā, Pūtanā, Mātṛkās and others; Unmādas (spirits causing insanity,) and Apasmāras which trouble the body, senses and vital breaths; the evil spirits that frighten in dreams, the great calamitous portents, and those wicked spirits that seize the old and the young all these which are terrified at the mention of Viṣṇu’s name—may all these wicked spirits be destroyed.”

devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । इति प्रणयबद्धाभिः गोपीभिः कृतरक्षणम् । पाययित्वा स्तनं माता संन्यवेशयदात्मजम् ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvāca iti praṇayabaddhābhiḥ gopībhiḥ kṛtarakṣaṇam pāyayitvā stanaṃ mātā saṃnyaveśayadātmajam

[imperative active second plural], [injunctive middle third single]

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
When the safety of the child was secured in this way (by performing the above-mentioned rites) by the Gopa women, bound to him with ties of affection, the mother suckled him and put him to bed.

devanagari: तावन्नन्दादयो गोपा मथुराया व्रजं गताः । विलोक्य पूतनादेहं बभूवुः अतिविस्मिताः ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: tāvannandādayo gopā mathurāyā vrajaṃ gatāḥ vilokya pūtanādehaṃ babhūvuḥ ativismitāḥ
translation: In the meanwhile, Nanda and other Gopas (cowherds) reached Vraja from Mathurā. They became extremely astonished to see the (enormous) body of Pūtanā.

devanagari: नूनं बतर्षिः सञ्जातो योगेशो वा समास सः । स एव दृष्टो ह्युत्पातो यद् आहानकदुन्दुभिः ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: nūnaṃ batarṣiḥ sañjāto yogeśo vā samāsa saḥ sa eva dṛṣṭo hyutpāto yad āhānakadundubhiḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: (They said to each other) “How wonderful! Certainly a veritable seer or a master of Yoga (in his previous incarnation) must have been born (in the form of Vasudeva). For that very calamity has been seen by us as was predicted by Vasudeva.

devanagari: कलेवरं परशुभिः छित्त्वा तत्ते व्रजौकसः । दूरे क्षिप्त्वावयवशो न्यदहन् काष्ठवेष्टितम् ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: kalevaraṃ paraśubhiḥ chittvā tatte vrajaukasaḥ dūre kṣiptvāvayavaśo nyadahan kāṣṭhaveṣṭitam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative single from √ veṣṭ class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ veṣṭ ]
[nominative single from √ veṣṭ class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ veṣṭ class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ veṣṭ ], [accusative single from √ veṣṭ ]

translation: (Then) the inhabitants of Vraja hacked down that (Pūtanā’s) body with axes and threw the pieces at a distance and burnt it limb by limb on pyres of logs of wood.

devanagari: दह्यमानस्य देहस्य धूमश्चागुरुसौरभः । उत्थितः कृष्णनिर्भुक्त सपद्याहतपाप्मनः ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: dahyamānasya dehasya dhūmaścāgurusaurabhaḥ utthitaḥ kṛṣṇanirbhukta sapadyāhatapāpmanaḥ
translation: There arose (a column of) smoke fragrant like that of aguru sandle-wood, out of that burning body, the sins of which were instantaneously annihilated, the moment Kṛṣṇa sucked (the life out of) it.

devanagari: पूतना लोकबालघ्नी राक्षसी रुधिराशना । जिघांसयापि हरये स्तनं दत्त्वाप सद्‍गतिम् ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: pūtanā lokabālaghnī rākṣasī rudhirāśanā jighāṃsayāpi haraye stanaṃ dattvāpa sad‍gatim
translation: (How wonderful!) Pūtanā, the blood-sucking demoness, the infant-killer in this world attained to Mokṣa by giving suck to him, despite her intention of killing him.

devanagari: किं पुनः श्रद्धया भक्त्या कृष्णाय परमात्मने । यच्छन्प्रियतमं किं नु रक्तास्तन्मातरो यथा ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: kiṃ punaḥ śraddhayā bhaktyā kṛṣṇāya paramātmane yacchanpriyatamaṃ kiṃ nu raktāstanmātaro yathā

[nominative plural from √ rañj class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ rañj class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ rañj class 1 verb], [nominative plural from √ rañj class 4 verb], [vocative plural from √ rañj class 4 verb], [accusative plural from √ rañj class 4 verb]

translation: How much more would they who dedicate with faith and devotion their most beloved objects to Kṛṣṇa, the Supreme Lord, attain to Mokṣa as did his deeply attached mothers.[4]
purport:
Footnote [4]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā explains the plural form ‘mothers’ when Kṛṣṇa had actually two mothers—Yaśodā and Rohiṇī—by referring to Ch. 13 infra in which Kṛṣṇa is said to have assumed the forms of cowherd boys and calves when god Brahmā stole them away for one year and Kṛṣṇa in these forms was sucked by the Gopa women (the mothers of his abducted companions) and by cows (of those stolen calves). Thus those innumerable Gopa-women and cows became ipso facto his mothers.

devanagari: पद्‍भ्यां भक्तहृदिस्थाभ्यां वन्द्याभ्यां लोकवन्दितैः । अङ्गं यस्याः समाक्रम्य भगवान् अपिबत् स्तनम् ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: pad‍bhyāṃ bhaktahṛdisthābhyāṃ vandyābhyāṃ lokavanditaiḥ aṅgaṃ yasyāḥ samākramya bhagavān apibat stanam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [instrumental dual from √ vand class 1 verb], [dative dual from √ vand class 1 verb], [ablative dual from √ vand class 1 verb]
[instrumental dual from √ vand class 1 verb], [dative dual from √ vand class 1 verb], [ablative dual from √ vand class 1 verb]
[instrumental dual from √ vand class 1 verb], [dative dual from √ vand class 1 verb], [ablative dual from √ vand class 1 verb]

translation: When the Lord traversed Pūtanā’s body with his feet which are enshrined in the hearts of his devotees, and are adorable to those (gods like Śiva and Brahmā) who are themselves worshipped by people, and sucked her breast, she, in spite of her being a demoness, attained to heaven, the destination (after death) of his mothers. What more need be said of those cows and mothers (Gopa women) the milk from whose breasts and udders was sucked (so fondly) by Kṛṣṇa? They must definitely attain to his abode.

devanagari: यातुधान्यपि सा स्वर्गं अवाप जननीगतिम् । कृष्णभुक्तस्तनक्षीराः किमु गावोऽनुमातरः ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: yātudhānyapi sā svargaṃ avāpa jananīgatim kṛṣṇabhuktastanakṣīrāḥ kimu gāvo
translation: When the Lord traversed Pūtanā’s body with his feet which are enshrined in the hearts of his devotees, and are adorable to those (gods like Śiva and Brahmā) who are themselves worshipped by people, and sucked her breast, she, in spite of her being a demoness, attained to heaven, the destination (after death) of his mothers. What more need be said of those cows and mothers (Gopa women) the milk from whose breasts and udders was sucked (so fondly) by Kṛṣṇa? They must definitely attain to his abode.

devanagari: पयांसि यासामपिबत् पुत्रस्नेहस्नुतान्यलम् । भगवान् देवकीपुत्रः कैवल्याद्यखिलप्रदः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: payāṃsi yāsāmapibat putrasnehasnutānyalam bhagavān devakīputraḥ kaivalyādyakhilapradaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ snu class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ snu class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ snu class 2 verb]

translation: For the glorious Lord, the son of Devakī, the dispenser of Final Emancipation and all other blessings, drank to his heart’s content the milk flowing (out of their breasts) through maternal affection.

devanagari: तासामविरतं कृष्णे कुर्वतीनां सुतेक्षणम् । न पुनः कल्पते राजन् संसारोऽज्ञानसंभवः ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: tāsāmavirataṃ kṛṣṇe kurvatīnāṃ sutekṣaṇam na punaḥ kalpate rājan saṃsāro

translation: They who have incessantly looked upon (and thus meditated upon) him as their own son, shall never revert to Saṃsāra, the cause of which is ignorance.

devanagari: कटधूमस्य सौरभ्यं अवघ्राय व्रजौकसः । किमिदं कुत एवेति वदन्तो व्रजमाययुः ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: kaṭadhūmasya saurabhyaṃ avaghrāya vrajaukasaḥ kimidaṃ kuta eveti vadanto vrajamāyayuḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ vad class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ vad class 1 verb]

translation: Having smelt the fragrance of the smoke issuing from the funeral pyre (of Pūtanā), the inhabitants of Vraja (who were then away from home) returned to Vraja enquiring, “What it is this (fragrance) due to? Whence is it coming?”

devanagari: ते तत्र वर्णितं गोपैः पूतना गमनादिकम् । श्रुत्वा तन्निधनं स्वस्ति शिशोश्चासन् सुविस्मिताः ॥ ४२ ॥
english_devnagari: te tatra varṇitaṃ gopaiḥ pūtanā gamanādikam śrutvā tannidhanaṃ svasti śiśoścāsan suvismitāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ varṇ class 10 verb], [accusative single from √ varṇ class 10 verb]

translation: Hearing from the cowherds the account of the arrival of Pūtanā etc., her death and the safety of the child, they were extremely wonder-struck.

devanagari: नन्दः स्वपुत्रमादाय प्रेत्यागतमुदारधीः । मूर्ध्न्युपाघ्राय परमां मुदं लेभे कुरूद्वह ॥ ४३ ॥
english_devnagari: nandaḥ svaputramādāya pretyāgatamudāradhīḥ mūrdhnyupāghrāya paramāṃ mudaṃ lebhe kurūdvaha

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

translation: Taking up the child (in his arms) as if he had returned from the jaws of death, the noble-minded Nanda smelt him on the crown of his head, and was overwhelmed with joy, Oh best of Kurus.

devanagari: य एतत्पूतनामोक्षं कृष्णस्यार्भकमद्‍भुतम् । श्रृणुयात् श्रद्धया मर्त्यो गोविन्दे लभते रतिम् ॥ ४४ ॥
english_devnagari: ya etatpūtanāmokṣaṃ kṛṣṇasyārbhakamad‍bhutam śrṛṇuyāt śraddhayā martyo govinde labhate ratim
translation:
A man who will listen with faith this wonderful account of the liberation of Pūtanā, a sportive achievement of Lord Kṛṣṇa in his childhood, will develop love and devotion to Govinda.
